message.content: You are a security reviewer. Review /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/src/ and the workflows in /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/.github/workflows/ for security issues: (1) command construction — can config values (target, labels, image) inject arguments into gh/docker invocations? Note std::process::Command passes args as vectors (no shell), but leading-dash argument injection into docker/gh flags is still possible — check each. (2) JIT config exposure via docker inspect / process listing — evaluate the documented tradeoff honestly. (3) Runner container hardening: what's missing (network egress, read-only rootfs, seccomp)? Is no-new-privileges + cgroup limits + no docker.sock accurate per the code? (4) Public-repo self-hosted runner risk: selftest.yml is workflow_dispatch-only — is anything else triggerable by forks? Is CI (ci.yml) on ubuntu-latest (safe)? (5) service.rs: unit/plist content injection via exe path? Report findings with severity and concrete attack scenarios.

[REDACTED for publication: verbatim content from a private repository (worldarchitect.ai self-hosted runner infrastructure) was returned by this tool call and has been removed. The reviewer's analysis of it is preserved in subsequent assistant messages.]

1	# E2E self-test: runs on an ezgha-managed ephemeral runner.
2	# Dispatch-only on purpose — never triggered by fork PRs (self-hosted safety).
3	name: ezgha-selftest
4	on:
5	  workflow_dispatch:
6	
7	permissions:
8	  contents: read
9	
10	jobs:
11	  selftest:
12	    runs-on: [self-hosted, ezgha]
13	    timeout-minutes: 10
14	    steps:
15	      - name: Prove execution environment
16	        run: |
17	          echo "runner name: $RUNNER_NAME"
18	          echo "hostname: $(hostname)"
19	          uname -a
20	          nproc
21	          cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.max 2>/dev/null || cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/memory.limit_in_bytes 2>/dev/null || echo "no cgroup file"
22	          cat /sys/fs/cgroup/pids.max 2>/dev/null || true
23	          echo "ephemeral runner proof: this job ran, the runner will now deregister"
24

1	name: CI
2	on:
3	  push:
4	    branches: [main]
5	  pull_request:
6	
7	permissions:
8	  contents: read
9	
10	jobs:
11	  test:
12	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
13	    steps:
14	      - uses: actions/checkout@08c6903cd8c0fde910a37f88322edcfb5dd907a8 # v5.0.0
15	        with:
16	          persist-credentials: false
17	      - name: Build
18	        run: cargo build --all-targets
19	      - name: Test
20	        run: cargo test
21	      - name: Clippy
22	        run: cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings
23	      - name: Format
24	        run: cargo fmt --check
25

content.text: I've reviewed all six source files and both workflows. Before reporting, one factual check on the documented tradeoff claim.

content.content: [REDACTED for publication: verbatim content from a private repository (worldarchitect.ai self-hosted runner infrastructure) was returned by this tool call and has been removed. The reviewer's analysis of it is preserved in subsequent assistant messages.]

<tool_use_error>InputValidationError: StructuredOutput was called with input that could not be parsed as JSON.
You sent (first 200 of 1685 bytes): {"findings": [
  {
    "title": "Runner containers have unrestricted network egress and default capabilities",
    "severity": "major",
    "detail": "In /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/src/dock
Common causes: unescaped backslashes in file paths (use / or \\), unescaped control characters, or truncated output. Retry with valid JSON.</tool_use_error>

findings.title: Runner image field allows leading-dash argument injection into `docker run`, defeating all hardening flags
findings.detail: In src/docker_backend.rs:87 the configured image is appended as a bare positional (`cmd.arg(&cfg.runner.image)`) after all hardening flags and before `./run.sh`. `docker run` parses flags up to the first non-flag positional (the IMAGE), so a value that begins with `-` is consumed as an option rather than an image name. Because each value is a single argv element, curated flags like `--privileged`, `--network=host`, `--user=0:0`, `--cap-add=ALL`, `-v=/:/host`, or `--entrypoint=/bin/sh` all parse successfully; `./run.sh` then becomes the IMAGE and `--jitconfig <jit>` the command. There is no validation on `cfg.runner.image` and no way to insert a `--` guard (docker run does not support `--` before IMAGE). Attack scenario: an org-distributed or lower-privilege-written config sets image="--privileged"; the next spawned runner runs privileged with host device access, silently nullifying no-new-privileges, cgroup limits, and container isolation — the tool's entire security premise. The same class applies weakly to github target (src/github.rs:10-15) but that is always prefixed with `repos/`/`orgs/` so it cannot become a leading-dash flag (at most path-segment manipulation).
findings.recommendation: Validate that `cfg.runner.image` (and defensively `target` and each `label`) does not start with `-` and matches an expected image-reference shape before use; reject at config load and at start_one(). Consider pinning image by digest and warning on any value not matching a registry/name[:tag|@digest] pattern.

findings.title: Runner containers omit capability drop, network egress restriction, and read-only rootfs
findings.detail: src/docker_backend.rs:79-86 sets --memory/--memory-swap/--cpus/--pids-limit and --security-opt no-new-privileges (plus sysbox runtime when present), but nothing else. Missing: (1) --cap-drop ALL — no-new-privileges only blocks *gaining* new privileges via setuid; it does NOT drop the container's default Linux capability set (CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, NET_RAW, SETUID, SETGID, etc.), so a job retains those. (2) No --network / egress control — the runner has full outbound network and LAN/host-service reachability by default; a compromised job can exfiltrate or pivot. (3) No --read-only rootfs (would need tmpfs for the runner work dir, so it's a larger change, but currently the entire container fs is writable). (4) No explicit --user enforcement (relies on the image's runner uid 1001) and no custom seccomp/AppArmor beyond docker defaults (docker's default seccomp IS applied, which is acceptable). The README 'Security notes' imply a hardened container but do not mention that capabilities are retained, giving a false sense of completeness. Attack scenario: a job on a self-hosted runner that runs partially-trusted code uses retained NET_RAW/default caps plus unrestricted egress to scan the internal network or exfiltrate secrets, none of which the current flags prevent.
findings.recommendation: Add `--cap-drop ALL` (adding back only capabilities run.sh genuinely needs), constrain egress (e.g. a dedicated docker network with firewall rules, or --network none where the job model allows), and evaluate --read-only with explicit --tmpfs mounts for the work dir. Update README/DESIGN to state that capabilities and egress are/aren't restricted so the posture claims match the code.

findings.title: JIT registration credential is exposed on the command line (docker inspect / /proc/*/cmdline) with only a documented 'single-user host' caveat
findings.detail: src/docker_backend.rs:88 passes the base64 JIT config as `./run.sh --jitconfig <jit>`. The JIT config is a bearer credential embedding the ephemeral runner's registration/auth; anyone who reads it during the window before the job is picked up can impersonate the runner and receive that job's assignment (source checkout + workflow secrets). It is visible via `docker inspect` (Cmd/Args), the host `docker run` process argv, and `/proc/<pid>/cmdline` for run.sh inside the container. DESIGN.md:81-82 acknowledges this as an 'acceptable single-user-host tradeoff — it is single-use and expires.' The honest evaluation: the mitigation is real but the 'single-user host' assumption is load-bearing and unenforced — the tool also supports org scope and multi-tenant hosts where a co-resident (even unprivileged) process could race to steal the credential. It is single-use and short-lived, which bounds the window, but the argv exposure is strictly worse than a file/env path. Note GitHub's run.sh only accepts --jitconfig as an argument, so the arg form is partly forced.
findings.recommendation: Keep the tradeoff but tighten it: (1) prominently document that the argv exposure means the host must be treated as single-tenant/trusted, not just 'single-user'; (2) investigate delivering the jitconfig via a tmpfs-mounted file that run.sh reads, or an env var, to remove it from argv/inspect where the runner image supports it; (3) ensure logs never echo the jit value.

findings.title: service.rs interpolates the executable path into systemd unit and launchd plist without escaping/validation
findings.detail: src/service.rs builds the systemd unit with `ExecStart={} serve` (line 33) and the launchd plist with `<string>{}</string>` (line 71) from `std::env::current_exe()`, with no escaping. Linux filenames may contain any byte except '/' and NUL, including newlines, spaces, and XML metacharacters. A binary installed at a path containing a newline could inject additional systemd directives (e.g. a second ExecStart/ExecStartPre); a path containing `<`/`>`/`</string>` could inject extra ProgramArguments into the plist; a path with spaces silently breaks systemd ExecStart tokenization. Real severity is limited because controlling the binary's path already implies the ability to place/replace the binary, so this is defense-in-depth/robustness rather than a fresh privilege boundary. Still a genuine content-injection gap.
findings.recommendation: XML-escape the exe path before inserting into the plist, reject paths containing control characters/newlines, and quote the systemd ExecStart path (systemd supports quoting) so paths with spaces work correctly. Fail closed if the resolved exe path contains suspicious characters.

input.summary: Reviewed all 6 Rust sources and both workflows. No shell is used (Command arg-vectors), and gh label/name args are safely constructed with -f. Public-repo posture is sound: selftest.yml is workflow_dispatch-only and ci.yml runs on ubuntu-latest with pull_request (not pull_request_target), so forks never reach a self-hosted runner. Four findings: (1) MAJOR — cfg.runner.image is a bare positional in `docker run`, so a leading-dash value (e.g. --privileged, --network=host, -v=/:/host) is parsed as a docker flag and silently defeats every hardening flag; needs input validation (docker run has no `--` guard before IMAGE). (2) MAJOR — container hardening omits `--cap-drop ALL` (default caps are retained; no-new-privileges does not drop them), network egress restriction, and read-only rootfs; the no-new-privileges + cgroup + no-docker.sock claims are accurate but the README overstates completeness. (3) MAJOR — the JIT credential is passed on the command line (visible to docker inspect / /proc cmdline); the documented 'single-user host' tradeoff is honest but the assumption is unenforced and load-bearing, especially for org scope. (4) MINOR — service.rs interpolates the exe path into the systemd unit and launchd plist without XML-escaping/validation (content-injection, gated by binary-path control). Files: src/docker_backend.rs, src/github.rs, src/service.rs, src/config.rs.
